free_text: 236U resonance parameters at 5.467 eV from neutron
transmission measurements using thin liquid samples
This RPI-2014 transmission experiment used three
(i.e., thickness) of 236U. The thickness for each
sample was chosen to produce a non-saturated
transmission resonance at 5.467 eV. The thickest
sample provided a minimum transmission of
performed at the 15 m flight station. The RPI

The RPI-2014 resonance parameters were calculated
with the Total Monte Carlo. The RPI-2014 fission
width, Gammaf, was fixed at the ENDF-7.1 value
without any uncertainty. The NJOY code was used to
calculate the peak cross section of 236U at 5.467 eV,
No details on sources of uncertainties,
The RPI-2014 negative resonance parameter values for
236U were fitted with SAMMY while keeping the
resonance parameters for the 5.467 eV resonance fixed
to the RPI-2014 resonance values. All resonance
parameters from higher energy were taken from
ENDF-7.1. This method preserved the thermal cross
section values of ENDF-7.1 to within 0.5%.
The RPI-2014 value was calculated using ENDF-7.1
resonance parameters for 236U, while substituting the
RPI-2014 values for the 5.467 eV resonance and the
values for the negative energy resonance. The neutron
capture resonance integral was calculated using a
combination of the NJOY and INTER codes.
The uncertainty on the resonance integral
was found by application of the Total Monte Carlo
(TMC) method. The NJOY and INTER calculations were
performed repeatedly while randomly sampling each of
the RPI-2014 236U resonance parameters from a
Gaussian distribution. The width of each Gaussian
distribution was defined as the uncertainty on the
resonance parameter of interest. The reported
uncertainty on the capture resonance integral is the
standard deviation of the results. The calculations
were repeated 2000 times to ensure that the standard
deviation of the capture resonance integral converged
The 236U resonance parameters at 5.467 eV were used
to construct the total cross section using the NJOY
code. The cross section was Doppler broadened to a
temperature of 300 K and the peak value was obtained.
